title: Singin' hinnies
subtitle: Northumbrian griddle cakes, rich with lard and currants
excerpt: Northumbrian griddle cakes made with lard and currants, cooked on a dry griddle until the currants start to sing in the fat, hence the name. Quick to mix and quicker to cook. Makes eight cakes.
sourceNotes: Florence White, Good Things in England (1932): singin' hinnies are documented as a Northumberland and County Durham griddle cake. The name is said to come from the sizzle the currants make in the hot fat on the griddle, which sounds like singing. White's recipe uses lard and currants on a dry griddle, which is the form recorded here. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: regional griddle cakes of the north of England are noted alongside Welsh cakes as a household standard.

content.text: Singin' hinnies take their name from the noise the currants make when they hit the hot griddle, a low, fat sizzle that sounds like humming. They are a Northumberland griddle cake, richer than a scone and denser than a Welsh cake, and they go directly onto a dry pan without a trace of butter or oil.

Lard gives the characteristic flavour. Cold butter is a reasonable substitute but produces a slightly different result. The griddle temperature is medium, not high: the cakes need time to cook through without burning the outside. Watch the edges for the moment they turn matte, which means the dough is cooked to the centre.

items.name: Lard
items.prepNote: cold, cubed; or cold unsalted butter

items.name: Whole milk
items.prepNote: cold, plus a little more if needed

content.text: Whisk together the {{plain-flour}}, {{baking-powder}}, and {{sea-salt-fine}} in a large bowl.

the cold {{lard}} until the mixture looks like fine breadcrumbs. Stir in the {{currants}}. Add the {{whole-milk}} a little at a time, stirring with a fork, until a soft but not sticky dough forms. Use just enough milk to bring it together.

On a lightly floured surface, roll the dough to about 8 mm thick. Cut into rounds with a 7 to 8 cm cutter, or into squares with a knife. You should get eight cakes from the first roll; gather the scraps and re-roll once for the rest.
Cook on the griddle, 6 to 8 minutes per batch

Heat the griddle or a heavy frying pan over medium heat. Do not add fat. When a pinch of flour flicked onto the surface just turns pale golden in about 30 seconds, the pan is ready. Cook the hinnies for 3 to 4 minutes per side, until they are deep golden and the edges look dry and matte. The currants will sizzle. Press the centre of one: it should feel firm, not doughy.
Eat warm, split and buttered. They do not keep especially well, best within a couple of hours of the griddle.
